title: Grass Stains on Cotton
stainChemistry: Fresh grass contains chlorophyll (porphyrin pigment), carotenoid pigments, and plant proteins. Chlorophyll is a magnesium-porphyrin complex that bonds to cotton fibers through hydrogen bonding. Grass stains are water-soluble but the chlorophyll pigment itself is stubborn—it requires solvent (IPA) or enzyme to dislodge. White cotton is ideal for aggressive treatment: bleach, enzymes, heat, and solvents are all safe.
whyThisWorks: Enzyme breaks down plant protein bonds first. Alcohol dissolves chlorophyll pigment out of the fiber. H₂O₂ oxidizes any remaining green to colorless. Bleach finishes the job on white cotton.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Brush or wipe away dried grass particles before any water. Fresh grass particles will smear if wet first. Dry brushing removes 20-30% of loose particles. Don't scrub hard—light brushing is enough. Shake the garment outdoors to dislodge residue.

Rinse under cold running water to flush away loose chlorophyll. Blot with white towels (not colored—chlorophyll transfers). Cold water prevents chlorophyll from oxidizing to brown (which happens with hot water). This rinse removes the initial bright green stain. Some staining will remain—this is where enzyme and alcohol come in.

Mix Protein per product directions with warm water. White cotton tolerates warmth. Submerge stained area. Enzymes break down the plant protein bonds that hold chlorophyll to the fiber. After 15 minutes, gently agitate or brush with a soft brush. You'll see the green lighten as protein fragments release. This step is crucial—it loosens the chlorophyll pigment so alcohol can dissolve it out.

After enzyme, apply IPA directly to the stained area or soak in alcohol solution. Alcohol dissolves chlorophyll pigment out of the fiber. You'll see the stain visibly fade and the alcohol turn greenish as it pulls pigment. Work the alcohol in gently with a cloth. This is the key step for grass—alcohol is what actually removes the chlorophyll, not water. For white cotton, strong alcohol (70-90%) is safe.

Flush thoroughly with NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) to remove all chemistry residue. Work from back of fabric. Blot — do not rub. All oxidizer and enzyme residue must be fully removed before drying.

spottingProtocol.technique: Rinse thoroughly, then machine wash hot
spottingProtocol.dwellTime: Rinse until clear; wash hot with detergent
spottingProtocol.instruction: Rinse thoroughly under cold running water until all enzyme, alcohol, peroxide, and H₂O₂ for white/light fabrics only. Any leftover chemistry will cause yellowing. Then machine wash on hot water (65-95°C) with regular detergent. Hot wash removes alcohol residue and any final traces of pigment.

spottingProtocol.technique: Machine dry or line dry in sun
spottingProtocol.instruction: Machine dry on high heat is fine for white cotton. Or hang in direct sunlight—UV light has H₂O₂ for white/light fabrics only. Heat won't re-set grass stains on cotton.

homeSolutions.instruction: Test on hidden area first to ensure fabric tolerates the solutions.
Place fabric stain-side down on glass work surface. Grass is a combination stain (dry + wet) requiring two approaches.

PROCEDURE 1 (Dry-based): Apply dry-cleaning solvent (lighter fluid) to the stain. Rub gently with the back of a spoon from center outward. Apply petroleum jelly and continue rubbing. Rinse with dry-cleaning solvent.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Ensure good ventilation when using dry-cleaning solvent. Avoid skin contact.

homeSolutions.instruction: If grass remains after Procedure 1, apply detergent/vinegar solution (1/4 tbsp mild laundry detergent + 2 oz white vinegar + 2 oz cool water). Rub with spoon, then rinse with cool water.
Apply hydrogen peroxide 3% mixed with ammonia (equal parts) and allow to sit for 3 minutes to oxidize remaining grass chlorophyll.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Test on hidden area first — peroxide may lighten some dyes.

homeSolutions.instruction: Apply white vinegar to neutralize and remove any rings. Rinse again with cool water. Wipe rings outward from center.
If grass stain persists or if fabric is delicate, call a professional cleaner.

materialWarnings: AVOID hot water initially—causes chlorophyll oxidation to brown
DO NOT use water alone—water-based treatment won't dissolve chlorophyll
ALCOHOL may bleach colored fibers—test first if garment has colored trim
escalation: If grass staining persists after home enzyme and alcohol treatment, professional dry cleaners use professional solvents (NSD, petroleum spirits) to dissolve stubborn chlorophyll. For very old grass stains that have oxidized to brown, professionals may use reducing bleach first.

household.name: IPA (70-90%)
household.note: Dissolves chlorophyll pigment. This is the critical step for grass. Apply after enzyme.

household.name: 3% H₂O₂
household.note: Oxidizes residual chlorophyll to colorless. Fast (5 min). Safe for white.

household.name: Clorox Bleach
household.note: For white cotton ONLY. Dilute 1:10. Fastest grass removal (5-10 min).

professional.name: Protein (concentrated)
professional.note: Professional enzyme. Faster protein breakdown than household.

professional.name: Mulsolite (NSD) or Petroleum Spirits
professional.note: Professional solvents for stubborn chlorophyll. More effective than IPA.

customerExplanation: Grass is chlorophyll (plant pigment) bonded to plant proteins. Enzyme breaks the protein bonds. Alcohol dissolves the pigment out. White cotton tolerates bleach too if needed. Enzyme + alcohol = the most effective combination for grass.
commonMistakes: Using only water—chlorophyll won't dissolve in water alone. Alcohol is essential.
Using hot water—causes chlorophyll to oxidize to brown (harder to remove)
Skipping enzyme—enzyme loosens the chlorophyll so alcohol can dissolve it. Together they're powerful; apart, neither fully works.
Not rinsing completely—leftover alcohol will continue breaking down fibers and cause damage over time
scienceNote: Chlorophyll is a magnesium-centered porphyrin (Mg-tetrapyrrole). It bonds to cellulose through hydrogen bonding with the porphyrin's peripheral carboxyl groups. Heat (above 50°C) causes chlorophyll to oxidize to pheophytin (brown, chelated by zinc instead of magnesium), which is harder to remove. IPA disrupts hydrogen bonding and dissolves chlorophyll through nonpolar interactions with the porphyrin's hydrophobic core.
callPro: If grass stain persists after home enzyme and alcohol treatment, or if it has oxidized to brown, professional dry cleaners can use petroleum-based solvents (NSD, mineral spirits) that are more effective than IPA (IPA). For very old brown grass stains, professionals may use reducing bleach to reverse oxidation.
solventNote: Professional: Protein soak (1-2% solution, 40°C, 15-20 min), then Mulsolite (NSD) 50% dilution in warm water (40°C, 10-15 min) or petroleum spirits. Alcohol extraction preferred over water-based. Rinse with isopropyl to remove solvent residue, then final hot wash. For oxidized (brown) grass: consider sodium hydrosulfite (StreePene) soak first to reduce oxidized chlorophyll back to green, then enzyme and solvent.
